ingredients.name: Amylase
ingredients.notes: Targeted individual enzymes (protease, lipase, amylase, bromelain, papain, serrapeptase, lactase, cellulase, etc.) designed to break down specific food components in the GI lumen. Enzymes work locally — systemic absorption is NOT the relevant metric; GI stability and enzyme activity delivery matter. Without enteric coating, many enzymes are partially denatured by stomach acid (~30-50% survival). OTC enzyme supplements are not FDA-regulated and enzyme concentrations are not guaranteed (Johns Hopkins Medicine). Well-characterized branded forms (DigeZyme, ProHydrolase) have more reliable potency.

inactive_ingredients.name: Hypromellose
inactive_ingredients.notes: Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (hypromellose, E464) — plant-derived cellulose ether used as vegetarian/vegan capsule shell material and tablet coating agent. FDA GRAS. Inert, non-toxic, and non-allergenic. Provides moisture barrier and controlled-release properties. Brand names include Vcaps, Caplique.

inactive_ingredients.name: Silicon Dioxide
inactive_ingredients.notes: Amorphous silicon dioxide is the most common anti-caking agent in supplement manufacturing. FDA GRAS at ≤2%. EFSA 2018 data gap request was precautionary — no finding of harm. Low severity — tracked purely as non-nutritive excipient quality signal.
inactive_ingredients.mechanism_of_harm: Amorphous silicon dioxide (SiO2) used as anti-caking agent in tablets/capsules at <2% w/w. FDA GRAS (21 CFR 172.480, max 2% of food weight). EFSA 2018 re-evaluation could not confirm safety due to insufficient nanoparticle characterization data — this was a data gap, not a finding of harm. Amorphous SiO2 is poorly absorbed from the GI tract. Nanoparticle concerns are based on in vitro studies at concentrations far exceeding dietary exposure.
population_warnings: No specific population concerns at anti-caking agent levels (<2% w/w)
inactive_ingredients.harmful_notes: Amorphous silicon dioxide (SiO2) used as anti-caking agent in tablets/capsules at <2% w/w. FDA GRAS (21 CFR 172.480, max 2% of food weight). EFSA 2018 re-evaluation could not confirm safety due to insufficient nanoparticle characterization data — this was a data gap, not a finding of harm. warnings.safety_summary: An inert anti-caking agent used in tablets and capsules. FDA- and EFSA-approved at the levels used — low overall concern.

clinical_matches.effect_direction_rationale: Retained positive_strong classification; study_type=rct_single; evidence_level=ingredient-human; primary_outcome=Digestive Health; published_tags=RCT,clinical; registry_completed_trials_count=7; total_enrollment=168; endpoint_tags=digestive_health; notes/citations contain no automated downgrade signals.
clinical_matches.notes: Human evidence for digestive enzymes is strong in exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, but evidence for broad over-the-counter digestive support in otherwise healthy adults remains limited and product-specific.
clinical_matches.notable_studies: Pancreatic-enzyme replacement evidence is well established in pancreatic insufficiency and summarized in guidance and reviews such as PMID 33931040. Over-the-counter multi-enzyme blends have much less robust support; one placebo-controlled dyspepsia trial is indexed as PMID 37976892, but no broad general-population meta-analysis exists.

references_structured.title: Fear of COVID-19 and workplace phobia among Pakistani doctors: A survey study.
Efficacy of digestive enzyme supplementation in functional dyspepsia: A monocentric, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, clinical trial.
